title: Apple Lost the AI Race

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Yo, I was just thinking about all this AI race stuff and how everything's gone from zero to 100 and then Apple, they've just kind of been scrambling the past two years trying to build Apple Intelligence and haven't really done very well yet. I just feel like it's so obvious that they lost.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Won.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: What? No, they lost.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: No, they really won.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Wait, sorry, no. Are you trying to say that Apple, the one that announced the new Siri and then didn't deliver on it for two years until they had to start over and build it from the ground up with help from Google, that Apple won the AI race?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: See, I think your definition of the AI race is a little bit too narrow. Like obviously being OpenAI and having ChatGPT is one thing, but making the hardware that everyone runs AI on is a different thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Yes, but also I don't think you can ignore how hard Apple has lagged here. Like I remember when ChatGPT first came out and everybody saw this as like the obvious huge paradigm shift, so if you're a tech company you gotta move fast. So they did. Google starts going crazy, Meta turns the burners on, Bing was insane for a couple of weeks I remember all this action. And then Apple, just nothing. Just nothing from them. They literally refused to say AI in their events for months.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I know, but think about it, it's Apple, right? Their thing has never been being on the bleeding edge of tech. They always sit back, let the early adopters figure out what the pros and cons and the bugs are, and then they'll step in late with their own version taking it to the next level for their ecosystem, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Oh, oh, oh, that's what Apple Intelligence is? Apple waiting for the tech to get good enough for the first 100 million users of ChatGPT to work the bugs out? Like that can't be right.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But okay, obviously it's past the early adopter phase. But not every technology has to be adopted like some kind of race. Like obviously tech companies are trained to see emerging stuff as a threat.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But this AI stuff was never a threat to Apple's core business. Like you said it yourself, I watched your videos, they are the iPhone company and they sell the iPhone.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Okay, but you would expect still the company with trillions of dollars to spend would still see ChatGPT spin up and then Gemini and Claude and Meta and all this other stuff and would think, okay, number one, this is here to stay, and number two, we should build a version of our own that's cohesive with our ecosystem. But Apple Intelligence has been kind of a joke so far.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Okay, hot take. I don't think Apple Intelligence has to be that good.

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: Okay, but what if I'm not buying an iPhone? Like, what if I'm someone who really cares about AI? Then there's so many other phones that I would buy before an iPhone, right? I would buy a Google phone, or a Samsung phone, or a Xiaomi phone. They all have the AI assistants and the writing tools and the, I don't know, the photo editing, all this AI stuff that's way better than what Apple's been making.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: What?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Yeah, no, I basically think it just has to be good enough for investors to believe Apple's competitive or just doing something with AI, but it doesn't have to be amazing for Apple to still be winning. Because you're still buying an iPhone.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Yeah, but dude, everything you just mentioned is such a gimmick. Like no one's actually buying a new phone just to use that stuff, really. Like it almost feels like Apple's just playing along just to, you know, have some stuff that's comparable. Oh yeah, here's some writing tools, here's some photo editing with AI and then of course the Siri improvements, you know, Genmoji.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And Genmoji, of course. But here, tell me if you agree with this. All of the fastest, most secure generative AI stuff happens on device, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and then the bigger, more capable models, that's what you go to the cloud for.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Exactly. So as these on-device models get better and better, you go to the cloud less and less, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, in the future someday, you will basically never have to go to the cloud, you'll do all your stuff on device. And who's gonna make that device?

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: Apple.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Maybe. Honestly, I don't think maybe. I think definitely. Like, in this future where all these early adopters finally get their local on-device models doing tasks for them and running everything locally with tons of memory on these machines, Apple's gonna be there to sell those machines. That's where they fit into this AI puzzle. And just because they don't do the software doesn't really mean they're losing. Like, they don't do search engines either, and they sell you the device that you do the Googling on. So, yeah. What do you think Nvidia wants to make ChatRTX a thing so bad? They want a piece of that hardware pie too, especially outside the US.

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: Okay, so what I, what I think I hear you saying is there's actually two different races.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: In race number one, the competition is so far ahead, Apple's kind of already took the L. But they can't look like they're losing, so they have eventually cobbled together enough of an AI suite to not be embarrassing anymore. But I think it's pretty clear that in a world where company A pays company B a billion dollars a year, company B already won.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Fair enough.

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: But in race number two, the finish line is way out on the horizon and Apple does have a bit of a head start because they still make the iPhone.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And Apple is going to protect that iPhone's position with everything they've got. Now, they could still win that race in a way. But also, there's nothing to prevent OpenAI from coming along and making a phone and taking everything from Apple, the iPhone company. There are so many rumors of them working on something like that already.

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Okay, so Apple's going to enter a new product category and they're going to do it the exact same way they always do, which is by making it work the best with the iPhone. And actually, that's what they're doing with the—have you tried the beta, the new Apple Intelligence stuff? Like Siri pulls from iMessage and Apple Calendar and Photos in a way that the ChatGPT app cannot.

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: on the iPhone, never could. But Siri doesn't like excel at anything that ChatGPT or Gemini doesn't already do.

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: Like we're not coding apps or anything with Siri. It doesn't even work in the background, so you can't leave it and come back later for larger requests like you can in the others.

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: It also doesn't remember anything about you without making notes in the iPhone's Notes app. So it just has way less of that personal context. Like, that's just basic stuff you can already do today with the ChatGPT app on the iPhone.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Yeah, okay, so the real question is do you think an AI company's gonna come along and make some new hardware that's so good that I can get people to switch from the iPhone? Or do you believe Apple could make their AI good enough to keep people on the iPhone?

full_transcript.speaker: Andrew Manganelli
full_transcript.text: Hey, what y'all talking about?

highlights: Apple’s strategy often involves letting early adopters and competitors work through the initial bugs of a new technology before stepping in late with a polished, ecosystem-integrated version.

The AI market can be viewed as two distinct races: a software race where Apple has lagged behind pioneers like OpenAI, and a hardware race where Apple excels by providing the devices that run these models.

While third-party AI apps may be more capable in isolation, Apple’s "Apple Intelligence" gains a competitive advantage through deep integration with personal system data like iMessage, Calendar, and Photos.

As generative AI shifts from cloud-based processing to on-device execution for better speed and security, Apple is well-positioned to dominate because they control the hardware and silicon necessary for local computation.
summary:
This transcript features a debate between Marques Brownlee and Andrew Manganelli regarding Apple’s standing in the rapidly evolving "AI race." The discussion centers on whether Apple has failed by lagging behind innovators like OpenAI and Google, or if they are strategically positioned to win by controlling the hardware. While Andrew argues that Apple has clearly lost the initial software race—relying on partners and "cobbling together" features to avoid embarrassment—Marques contends that Apple’s strength lies in its "wait-and-see" approach, focusing on ecosystem integration rather than being on the bleeding edge.

The speakers distinguish between two different competitive arenas: the software race for the most powerful LLMs and the hardware race for on-device processing. They conclude that while Apple may have "taken the L" in raw AI software development, they are frontrunners in the hardware race. Because the future of AI is moving toward secure, local, on-device execution, Apple is uniquely positioned to sell the high-performance machines required to run these models.

Ultimately, the conversation highlights that "Apple Intelligence" doesn't necessarily need to be the most advanced AI to succeed; it just needs to be "good enough" to maintain user retention. By leveraging deep integration with personal data—like iMessages, Calendars, and Photos—Apple provides a level of contextual utility that third-party apps like ChatGPT cannot currently match. The overarching theme is a question of disruption: will an AI-first company eventually create hardware that replaces the iPhone, or will Apple’s hardware dominance keep them at the top of the food chain?
